preFix.title: Combined acoustic and thermal insulation for timber floors
preFix.subtitle: Choosing and fitting insulation that quiets impact noise and reduces heat loss together
preFix.excerpt: A timber floor between two occupied storeys has two jobs to do: slow heat loss through it and absorb impact and airborne noise passing between floors. Standard mineral wool batts serve both. Understanding which type to use, how thick, and how to avoid flanking paths means the same opening-up operation delivers a quieter and warmer house.
preFix.sourceNotes: Building Regulations Approved Document E (Resistance to the Passage of Sound) 2003, amended 2015. BS 8233:2014 guidance on sound insulation. CIBSE Guide A floor thermal performance data. NHBC Standards Chapter 8.1 for separating floor requirements.

content.text: The floor between a bedroom and a living room does two things: it slows heat movement between rooms, and it reduces sound transfer. These goals work together. A joist void filled with 100 mm of acoustic mineral wool (high-density rock wool or glass wool, not standard loft roll) reduces heat loss and cuts sound at the same time.
Choosing the right insulation

Not all mineral wool is equal for acoustic performance. The key parameter is density. Standard loft roll runs at 10 to 20 kg/m3 and has a sound absorption coefficient of around 0.7 at mid-frequencies. Acoustic slab (branded products from Rockwool, Knauf, or Isover in the 40 to 60 kg/m3 range) absorbs more efficiently. For a domestic floor between two rooms used simultaneously, 100 mm of acoustic slab fitted between joists improves
airborne sound
insulation by 6 to 8 dB compared with an empty void.

The thermal performance difference between acoustic slab and standard loft roll is small (lambda around 0.034 vs 0.044 W/mK). If the goal is primarily thermal, standard loft roll at lower cost is fine. If both goals matter equally, acoustic slab is worth the premium.
The flanking problem
Filling the joist void with insulation addresses direct sound transmission.
Flanking transmission

through the walls at the floor perimeter often dominates in practice, particularly in rigid masonry construction where the floor joists bear on a wall plate sitting on the inner leaf. Flanking can account for more than half of total sound transmission in a typical terraced house.

To reduce flanking at the floor perimeter, do three things. First, do not fix floorboards rigidly to the joists at the wall junction. Second, seal any service holes through the floor with acoustic sealant. Third, if the floor is already being opened, fit resilient bars under the ceiling below to decouple the plasterboard from the joists.
Installation method

Decide access direction. Lifting floorboards from above is less disruptive and allows checking the floor structure condition. Removing the ceiling below is sometimes unavoidable when the floorboards are fixed parquet, glued sheet, or structural concrete. For a typical 22 mm t&g board over Victorian joists, the lift-from-above method takes one day per room.

Lift boards and inspect. Once three or four boards are lifted, check for old debris, evidence of rot at joist ends, and any evidence of electrical or plumbing services running in the void. Cables and pipes that run across the void must be identified and not compressed by the insulation.

Support the insulation. Mineral wool batts need supporting from below so they do not sag and compress against the ceiling below (compressing reduces thermal performance). Fit netting, rigid netting, or tension wire in runs perpendicular to the joists at 400 to 600 mm intervals. Alternatively, nail battens to the sides of the joists 50 mm below the joist soffit to carry the insulation.

Fit the insulation. Cut the batts to the bay width (joist clear spacing) and lay them supported, filling the depth. For 200 mm joists and 100 mm acoustic slab, you have 100 mm of void above the insulation, useful for services. For a tighter void, reduce to a single 75 mm layer.

Seal service penetrations. Any pipe or cable penetration through the floor structure should be sealed with acoustic putty or mineral wool packing around the service, then capped with an escutcheon plate. A 25 mm gap around a soil pipe passing through the floor can transmit more sound than the whole uninsulated void.

Replace the floorboards. Re-fix at every joist crossing, not just at the ends. Squeaking after re-lay is almost always from boards rubbing against each other at the tongue; apply a thin bead of PVA to the tongue before driving the boards tight.
What to expect

In a 1930s semi, filling the joist void with 100 mm acoustic slab and sealing all penetrations reduces perceived impact noise by roughly 5 to 8 dB, halving the apparent loudness. Airborne noise falls by a similar amount. Footsteps become a dull thud rather than sharp taps. Normal conversation becomes hard to follow rather than clearly audible. Complete sound isolation requires a room-within-a-room build.
